refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting packet loss quality criteria. The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation (communication integrity) as the core functional requirement, removing the implicit 'support teleoperation' clause which is a higher-level system capability. The phrase 'communication quality meeting the customer-defined packet loss limit' is vague; 'maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits' is more precise and solution-neutral. The addition of 'during agreed network conditions' acknowledges the acceptance criterion context without prescribing how it is achieved.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation sessions under agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) and measure end-to-end packet loss rate across the communication channel. Verify that measured packet loss does not exceed the customer-defined threshold (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, payload types, and loss statistics.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall transmit teleoperation control commands from the tele-operator interface to the remote equipment with fidelity sufficient to preserve operator intent.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit telemetry and sensor data from the remote equipment to the tele-operator interface with fidelity sufficient to support situational awareness.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The NGHE solution shall detect and report communication loss events (packet loss, latency anomalies, or link failures) to the operator and safety system.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and selective retransmission based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency variance) to maintain command/feedback integrity within customer-defined thresholds.
DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent network paths and automatic failover logic to ensure continuous teleoperation control availability.
DP3: Deploy application-level forward error correction (FEC) encoding with configurable redundancy levels, tuned to customer network conditions, to recover lost packets without retransmission.
DP4: Implement a quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation and enforcement layer that prioritizes critical control commands and telemetry over non-critical data, with dynamic bandwidth allocation.

DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Use a hybrid approach combining lightweight heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment (SACK) of critical frames, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission to balance latency and reliability.

potential_sources: Heartbeat monitoring is decoupled from command/feedback payload processing—can be implemented as an independent timing layer.
SACK mechanism is orthogonal to payload encoding—operates at frame level without constraining application logic.
Adaptive timeout logic depends on network metrics (latency, loss rate) but does not mandate specific measurement technology; metrics can be sourced from multiple network layers.
Retransmission queue is logically independent from command generation and execution—clear separation of concerns.

Risk: If heartbeat interval and retransmission timeout are not carefully tuned, false-positive loss detection could trigger unnecessary retransmits, increasing congestion (minor coupling to network load).
coupling_check.notes: DP5 exhibits low functional coupling because each sub-mechanism (heartbeat, SACK, adaptive timeout, retransmission) can be designed, tested, and modified independently. The design is also solution-neutral: it does not prescribe UDP vs. TCP, specific codec, or vendor-specific protocols. Coupling risk is primarily operational (tuning sensitivity) rather than architectural.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Customer-defined packet loss target (≤1% or custom) is measurable and stable during agreed network conditions. Risk: If network conditions are highly variable or measurement is unreliable, adaptive tuning may oscillate or fail to converge.

Assumption: Teleoperation commands and feedback have well-defined criticality levels (e.g., emergency stop > position update > diagnostic telemetry). Risk: If criticality is ambiguous or changes dynamically, SACK prioritization logic may be incorrect.

Assumption: Retransmission buffer capacity and timeout windows can be sized without violating latency constraints (e.g., round-trip time + processing). Risk: If network RTT is very high or variable, adaptive timeout may cause unacceptable command delays.

Assumption: Heartbeat overhead (frequency, payload size) is negligible relative to available bandwidth. Risk: In severely bandwidth-constrained networks, heartbeat itself may contribute to congestion.

Assumption: Customer operational policies permit buffering and delayed delivery of non-critical telemetry. Risk: If all telemetry must be delivered in real-time, DP5's selective prioritization may violate safety or operational requirements.

Assumption: Network conditions remain within 'agreed' bounds during operation. Risk: If conditions degrade beyond agreement (e.g., sudden congestion spike), DP5 may not recover without manual intervention or policy override.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by ensuring that critical control commands and essential feedback are delivered with ≤ [customer-defined packet loss target, e.g., 1%] loss rate under agreed network conditions, while non-critical telemetry may be subject to selective delivery or buffering. The solution shall include configurable network quality monitoring and adaptive retransmission tuning to accommodate customer-specific network profiles and operational policies. DP5 is recommended because it balances reliability, latency, and architectural independence. To reduce risk, explicitly define: (1) criticality tiers for commands/feedback, (2) acceptable latency bounds for each tier, (3) network condition ranges (bandwidth, RTT, loss rate) for which DP5 is valid, and (4) fallback or alert mechanisms if conditions exceed agreement.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all remote control communication protocols, including heartbeat monitoring, selective acknowledgment of critical frames, adaptive timeout-driven retransmission, and latency/reliability trade-off logic for teleoperated vehicle commands and feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits heartbeat packets, SACK frames, and retransmitted critical commands; receives acknowledgments and network status feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and control inputs that require reliable delivery with adaptive timeout tuning.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports vehicle state and communication health metrics (latency, packet loss, retransmission counts) for monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals critical communication failures (e.g., heartbeat timeout, loss of SACK on safety-critical frames) to Safety Services.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: heartbeat, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are core teleoperation protocol concerns. Latency/reliability trade-off is inherent to remote control. Minor diffusion: safety thresholds may require coordination with Safety Services via SafetyAlert interface.

Poor fit: Vehicle module owns vehicle dynamics and actuation, not communication protocols. Heartbeat and SACK logic would be alien to vehicle state management. High diffusion: protocol logic would leak into command execution and feedback loops.

Weak fit: Safety Services owns safety policy and hazard mitigation, not communication protocol mechanics. Adaptive timeout and SACK are protocol-level concerns, not safety logic. Diffusion: would require Safety Services to own low-level network tuning.

Moderate fit if NGHE is a top-level integration layer. However, heartbeat/SACK/retransmission are specific to teleoperation, not a system-wide concern. Overly broad boundary; would dilute NGHE's role as orchestrator.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it is responsible for maintaining reliable, low-latency command and feedback channels between operator and vehicle. Heartbeat monitoring detects link loss, SACK ensures critical frames (e.g., emergency stop) are confirmed, and adaptive timeout-driven retransmission balances responsiveness with reliability—all core teleoperation concerns. The module's external interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) provide sufficient abstraction to isolate protocol mechanics from vehicle dynamics and safety policy.
containment_risks: Timeout adaptation logic may require feedback from Safety Services (e.g., stricter timeouts during high-risk maneuvers); ensure SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional or introduce a configuration input.

SACK frame selection (which frames are 'critical') may drift if safety requirements change; establish clear ownership of criticality rules (likely Safety Services) and expose as a configuration parameter to Teleoperation.

Heartbeat frequency and SACK acknowledgment latency thresholds may conflict with Vehicle module's actuation bandwidth; validate via integration testing and document constraints in Network Link interface specification.

If multiple teleoperation modes (e.g., direct control vs. supervised autonomy) require different protocol tuning, ensure Teleoperation module can switch strategies without leaking mode logic into Vehicle or Safety Services.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (packet loss ≤ 1% or customer-defined target under agreed network conditions). The Teleoperation IM boundary owns heartbeat monitoring, SACK, and adaptive retransmission—all directly observable and testable via network simulation, field trials, and telemetry export. Telemetry interface explicitly exports latency, packet loss, and retransmission counts, enabling continuous verification against acceptance criteria.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR requirement maps unambiguously to the Teleoperation module (containment_score 0.92). The module's scope explicitly includes all remote control communication protocols and latency/reliability trade-off logic. External interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) establish clean boundaries: Teleoperation owns protocol mechanics; Safety Services owns safety policy; Vehicle owns actuation. No ambiguity in ownership.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (hybrid heartbeat + SACK + adaptive timeout-driven retransmission) directly addresses the FR requirement to maintain communication integrity within packet loss limits. The approach is technically sound: heartbeat detects link loss, SACK confirms critical frames, adaptive timeout balances latency and reliability. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation and is traceable to the FR. No logical gaps or contradictions identified.

diffusion_risks: Timeout adaptation logic may require real-time feedback from Safety Services (e.g., stricter timeouts during high-risk maneuvers). Current SafetyAlert interface is unidirectional (out). Recommend introducing a bidirectional 'SafetyContext' input or explicit configuration parameter to allow Safety Services to influence timeout tuning without breaking module boundaries.

SACK frame criticality rules (which frames require acknowledgment) are currently owned implicitly by Teleoperation but should be explicitly owned by Safety Services. Risk: criticality rules may drift if safety requirements change without Teleoperation being notified. Recommend exposing criticality rules as a configuration input to Teleoperation, with ownership and change control assigned to Safety Services.

Heartbeat frequency and SACK acknowledgment latency thresholds may conflict with Vehicle module's actuation bandwidth and command execution cycles. Risk: protocol tuning may inadvertently degrade vehicle responsiveness. Recommend documenting constraints in Network Link interface specification and validating via integration testing (e.g., worst-case latency under maximum packet loss).

If multiple teleoperation modes (direct control vs. supervised autonomy) require different protocol tuning strategies, Teleoperation module must support mode switching without leaking mode logic into Vehicle or Safety Services. Risk: mode-specific timeout or SACK rules could scatter across modules. Recommend encapsulating mode-specific protocol profiles within Teleoperation and exposing mode selection as a Tasking input.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural owner of DP5 because heartbeat, SACK, and adaptive retransmission are core remote control protocol concerns, not vehicle dynamics or safety policy. The module's external interfaces provide sufficient abstraction: Network Link isolates protocol mechanics from physical network; Tasking and Telemetry decouple operator commands and vehicle feedback from protocol tuning; SafetyAlert enables safety-aware protocol behavior without embedding safety logic. Minor diffusion risk: safety thresholds and criticality rules require coordination with Safety Services. Recommend clarifying ownership of criticality rules and introducing a configuration input to Teleoperation to avoid implicit coupling.

recommended_revisions: Introduce a bidirectional 'SafetyContext' input interface (or extend SafetyAlert to include feedback) to allow Safety Services to dynamically adjust timeout and SACK thresholds based on operational risk level (e.g., high-risk maneuvers require stricter timeouts). Document the contract: Safety Services owns safety thresholds; Teleoperation owns protocol mechanics.

Explicitly assign ownership of SACK frame criticality rules to Safety Services. Expose criticality rules as a configuration input to Teleoperation (e.g., a list of command types or frame IDs that require acknowledgment). Document how criticality rules are updated and validated when safety requirements change.

Add a constraint specification to the Network Link interface documenting maximum acceptable heartbeat latency, SACK acknowledgment delay, and retransmission overhead under agreed network conditions. Include worst-case scenarios (e.g., 1% packet loss, 100 ms latency) and validate via integration testing with Vehicle module.

If multiple teleoperation modes are anticipated, document mode-specific protocol profiles within Teleoperation module design. Expose mode selection as a Tasking input parameter. Ensure mode switching does not require changes to Vehicle or Safety Services modules.

Enhance Telemetry interface to export protocol tuning parameters (current timeout, SACK threshold, heartbeat frequency) in addition to health metrics. This enables post-hoc analysis of protocol behavior under various network conditions and supports validation against acceptance criteria.

Define acceptance test plan: (1) Simulate agreed network conditions (packet loss ≤ 1%, latency ranges) and verify packet loss remains ≤ 1% with DP5 active; (2) Verify heartbeat detects link loss within acceptable time; (3) Verify SACK confirms critical frames; (4) Verify adaptive timeout does not degrade vehicle responsiveness; (5) Verify SafetyAlert triggers on protocol failures. Document test results and traceability to FR.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence, confirming that FR5 is well-formed and DP5 is technically sound. However, the DP-IM gate identifies four material diffusion risks that require explicit sub-FR decomposition to resolve: (1) safety-aware timeout adaptation requires bidirectional SafetyContext coupling; (2) SACK criticality rules ownership is implicit and must be formalized; (3) protocol tuning constraints must be validated against Vehicle actuation bandwidth; (4) multi-mode teleoperation requires encapsulated mode-specific profiles. These are not defects in the current level but rather architectural dependencies that must be made explicit and owned at the next decomposition level. Deriving sub-FRs will clarify ownership boundaries, formalize safety-protocol coordination, and enable testable acceptance criteria for each concern. High safety criticality reinforces the need for explicit decomposition rather than implicit coupling.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Adapt communication timeout and SACK thresholds based on operational safety context (risk level, maneuver type) provided by Safety Services, without embedding safety policy logic in Teleoperation module.
FR5.2: Manage SACK frame criticality rules (which command types or frame IDs require acknowledgment) as a configuration input owned and updated by Safety Services, with change control and validation.

FR5.3: Validate that heartbeat frequency, SACK acknowledgment latency, and retransmission overhead do not degrade Vehicle module's command execution responsiveness under agreed network conditions (packet loss ≤ 1%, latency ranges).

FR5.4: Support multiple teleoperation modes (direct control, supervised autonomy, or customer-defined variants) with mode-specific protocol tuning profiles encapsulated within Teleoperation module, exposed via Tasking input, without leaking mode logic to Vehicle or Safety Services.

revise.why: DP5 is technically sound but underspecifies the coordination mechanisms required to resolve diffusion risks. Recommend revising DP5 to explicitly include: (1) a SafetyContext input interface (or bidirectional SafetyAlert extension) that carries safety thresholds and risk levels; (2) a criticality rules configuration input that Safety Services owns and updates; (3) constraint specifications for heartbeat and SACK latency validated against Vehicle actuation cycles; (4) mode-specific protocol profile selection logic triggered by Tasking input. These additions do not change the core heartbeat + SACK + adaptive timeout logic but make the safety and mode dependencies explicit and testable. Revised DP5 should read: 'Implement hybrid heartbeat + SACK + adaptive timeout-driven retransmission with: (a) SafetyContext input to dynamically adjust timeout/SACK thresholds based on operational risk; (b) criticality rules configuration input owned by Safety Services; (c) mode-specific protocol profiles selectable via Tasking; (d) constraint validation against Vehicle actuation bandwidth and Network Link latency bounds.'

assumptions: Safety Services module exists and can provide bidirectional feedback (SafetyContext) to Teleoperation without violating module independence.
Criticality rules (which frames require SACK) are determined by safety requirements and can be externalized as a configuration input without requiring runtime changes to Teleoperation core logic.
Vehicle module's actuation bandwidth and command execution cycles are documented and available for constraint validation.
Multiple teleoperation modes (if required) can be represented as distinct protocol profiles without requiring conditional logic scattered across modules.
Network conditions (packet loss ≤ 1%, latency ranges) are agreed upon and stable during system operation; dynamic network condition changes are handled by adaptive timeout logic.
open_risks: SafetyContext interface design: if Safety Services provides real-time risk level feedback, Teleoperation must handle rapid threshold changes without destabilizing protocol state. Risk: oscillating timeouts or SACK thresholds could degrade reliability. Mitigation: define hysteresis or rate-limiting rules for threshold updates.

Criticality rules ownership: if Safety Services updates criticality rules dynamically (e.g., during mode transitions), Teleoperation must validate and apply changes atomically. Risk: partial rule updates could cause inconsistent frame handling. Mitigation: define versioning and atomic update semantics for criticality rules.

Vehicle actuation constraint validation: if Vehicle module's command execution cycles are tighter than Teleoperation's SACK acknowledgment latency, protocol tuning may be infeasible. Risk: unresolvable conflict between reliability and responsiveness. Mitigation: conduct early integration testing to identify conflicts; consider relaxing one constraint or introducing intermediate buffering.

Mode-specific profile encapsulation: if mode switching is frequent or overlapping modes are possible, Teleoperation must handle profile transitions safely. Risk: mode-specific state leakage or incomplete profile application. Mitigation: define explicit mode transition rules and validate via state machine testing.

Telemetry export completeness: if protocol tuning parameters are not exported, post-hoc analysis of protocol behavior under failure conditions will be limited. Risk: inability to diagnose why packet loss exceeded 1% in field conditions. Mitigation: ensure Telemetry interface exports all tuning parameters and raw protocol metrics.
evidence_needed: SafetyContext interface specification: define message format, update frequency, and latency requirements for safety threshold feedback. Include examples of how risk levels map to timeout/SACK adjustments.

Criticality rules configuration schema: define data structure (e.g., list of frame IDs, command types, or safety-tagged message categories) and versioning mechanism. Document how Safety Services updates rules and how Teleoperation validates and applies changes.

Vehicle actuation constraint document: specify command execution cycle time, maximum acceptable SACK acknowledgment latency, and heartbeat frequency constraints. Include worst-case scenarios (e.g., simultaneous high-frequency commands + 1% packet loss).

Mode-specific protocol profile definitions: if multiple modes are anticipated, document each profile (timeout values, SACK thresholds, heartbeat frequency, criticality rules) and mode transition rules. Include rationale for profile differences.

Integration test plan for FR5.1–FR5.4: define test scenarios for each sub-FR, including SafetyContext feedback injection, criticality rule updates, Vehicle actuation latency validation, and mode switching under network stress.

Telemetry export specification: confirm that protocol tuning parameters (current timeout, SACK threshold, heartbeat frequency, mode) and raw metrics (heartbeat count, SACK confirmations, retransmission count, detected link losses) are exported and timestamped for post-hoc analysis.
